pages.text: Hydration products and strength development of calcium hydroxide-based alkali-activated slag mortars Keun-Hyeok Yang a,⇑, Ah-Ram Cho a, Jin-Kyu Song b,1, Sang-Ho Nam c,2 a Department of Architectural Engineering, Kyonggi University, Suwon, Kyonggi-do, Republic of Korea b Department of Architectural Engineering, Chonnam National University, Gwangju, Chonnam, Republic of Korea c Department of Chemistry, Mokpo National University, Chonnam, Republic of Korea article info Article history: Received 18 July 2011 Received in revised form 29 September 2011 Accepted 17 October 2011 Available online 29 November 2011 Keywords: Alkali activated mortar GGBS Auxiliary activator Strength development Hydration products Microstructure Mixing proportions abstract To strengthen the practical application of the Ca(OH) 2-based alkali-activated (AA) ground granulated blast-furnace slag (GGBS) system, the hydration products, microstructural characteristics and compres- sive strength development of the AA GGBS mortars were examined for 3 pastes and 21 mortar mixes according to the different auxiliary activators and curing conditions. 7.5% Ca(OH) 2 was used for the main activator and either 1% Na 2SiO3 or 2% Na 2CO3 was added for an auxiliary activator. Test results revealed that the diffraction peaks and molar Si/Ca ratio of the calcium silicate hydrate (CSH) gels were higher for water curing than for air-dried curing and with the addition of auxiliary activators than with the use of the single Ca(OH) 2. Furthermore, the addition of auxiliary activators contributed to the stabilization of CSH gels at a long-term age, showing that the molar Si/Ca ratio in CSH gels increased with the hydration time. These growth and intensity of CSH gels were consistent with higher strength development. The strength gain ratio of water-cured mortars was lower at the early ages but higher at the long-term ages than that of the companion air-cured mortars. This trend was independent of the mixing proportions of the mortars and the type of auxiliary activators. Simple equations were also proposed to predict the strength development of Ca(OH) 2-based AA GGBS mortars considering the inﬂuencing parameters includ- ing the curing condition, the type of auxiliary activators and the mixing proportions. The predicted com- pressive strength of Ca(OH) 2-based AA GGBS mortars at different ages was in good agreement with the test results, though the 3-day compressive strength was underestimated. /C2112011 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Since the beginning of the 21st century, the development of a sustainable concrete technology has been considered as one of the most salient issues for concrete industries worldwide [1,2]. As a result, deliberate attempts to reduce the consumption of or- dinary Portland cement (OPC) in concrete production have been increasingly made because the CO 2-emission coefﬁcient of OPC from a life cycle assessment (LCA) is commonly evaluated to be 0.8–1.0 ton-CO 2/ton [3]. In addition, considerable efforts for safe and sound recycling of by-products such as ground granulated blast-furnace slag (GGBS) and ﬂy ash (FA) have contributed to practical application of OPC concrete partially replaced with those by-products. With these enthusiastic endeavors and investigations [4–7], alkali-activated (AA) binders have began to be particularly noticed as a sustainable cementitious material in terms of recy- cling of by-products and replacement of OPC, especially s

hydroxide (Ca(OH)2)-activated GGBS binder due to its low cost and good durability [6,9,10]. The Ca(OH) 2-activated GGBS binder gen- erally has an advantage in regard to a relatively relieved workabil- ity loss, while intermittently shows an unsoundness due to lime depending onto the curing condition and the dosage of Ca(OH) 2. The addition of Ca(OH) 2 above 10% relative to GGBS by weight can result in a considerable reduction of compressive strength of Ca(OH)2-based concrete at the long-term age [10]. In addition, reli- able test data are very rare on the strength development of Ca(OH)2-based AA GGBS concrete at different curing conditions, though it has been reported [11,12] that compressive strength of AA GGBS concrete is commonly higher by more than 20% for water curing than for air-dried curing. Hence, further investigations would be needed to enhance the stable strength development and reactivity of a Ca(OH) 2-activated GGBS system. In the present study, strength development and hydration prod- ucts of the Ca(OH) 2-activated GGBS system with auxiliary activa- tors were investigated in three groups in order to explore the practical application and limitation of the Ca(OH) 2-based AA GGBS mortars. For group I, the hydration products and microstructural characteristics of the Ca(OH) 2-based AA GGBS were primarily traced using X-ray diffraction (XRD) and energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) analyses combined with a scanning electron microscope (SEM) image. For groups II and III, the compressive strength devel- opment of Ca(OH) 2-based AA GGBS mortars was examined accord- ing to different curing conditions and mixing proportions in order to provide basic data for future development for the practical de- sign of the AA GGBS mortar and concrete. 2. Highlights of research Although, it is generally known that the Ca(OH) 2-based AA GGBS binder has an practical advantage in regard to a relatively re- lieved workability loss, stable strength development and econom- ical efﬁciency, available test data on the effect of auxiliary activators on the hydration products and strength development of Ca(OH)2-activated GGBS binder are very rare. The present study evaluates the effect of auxiliary activators on the relationship of hydration products and microstructural characteristics of the Ca(OH)2-acitvated AA GGBS on the compressive strength develop- ment of the binder. In addition, this investigation provides basic data and empirical equations for the practical mixing design of the Ca(OH) 2-based AA GGBS mortar. Based on test results we can conclude that the addition of auxiliary activators contributed to the stabilization of CSH gels at a long-term age, showing that the molar Si/Ca ratio in CSH gels increased with the hydration time. 3. Experimental details 3.1. Materials To produce a cementless binder, GGBS was incorporated with different alkaline inorganic materials. Calcium hydroxide (Ca(OH) 2) was selected as a primary activa- tor while sodium silicate (Na 2SiO3 composed of 50.2% Na2O and 45% SiO2, producing a molar ratio of 0.9), and sodium carbonate (Na 2CO3) were used for an auxiliary activator to stabilize the reactivity of the Ca(OH) 2-activated GGBS system. The GGBS was mainly composed of CaO of 45%, SiO2 of 31.5%, Al2O3 of 13.8% and MgO of 5.2%. Table 1 gives the molar ratios of the major elements in the GGBS determined by EDX analysis as follows: silica-to-alumina ratio of 2.3, calcium-to-alumina ratio of 4.52 and silica-to-calcium ratio of 0.51

Na2SiO3, and 2.54 and 37.9 lm, respectively, for Na 2CO3. Locally available sand was used for ﬁne aggregate in a saturated surface dry condition. The speciﬁc gravity, grading and maximum size of sand particles used were 2.54, 2.97 and 5 mm, respectively. 3.2. Mix proportions All test specimens were classiﬁed into three groups. For group I, three Ca(OH) 2- based AA GGBS pastes were prepared, as given in Table 2, to examine the hydration products and compressive strength development according to the addition of aux- iliary activators and curing conditions. Three types of GGBS binders with 7.5% Ca(OH) 2 as the primary activator were prepared: O-type without an auxiliary acti- vator, S-type with auxiliary 1% Na 2SiO3 and C-type with auxiliary 2% Na 2CO3. These additional amounts of the auxiliary activators were determined from the prelimin- ary tests on the compressive strength development of Ca(OH) 2-based AA GGBS mortar. No water-reducing admixture was added to the paste mixes. For group II, a total of 9 Ca(OH) 2-based AA mortar mixes using S-type or C-type binders were tested according to the variation of water-to-binder ( W/B) ratio, as gi- ven in Table 3, though 12 mortar specimens had been prepared. Mortar mixes had a ﬁne aggregate-to-binder ( S/B) ratio of 3.0. To meet the designed initial ﬂow of 180 ± 10 mm, a high-range water-reducing admixture, of which the dosage is lim- ited to be 2%, was added. In spite of the addition of 2% water-reducing admixture, very poor workability with impossible casting appeared in the mortar specimens when the W/B ratio was less than 30% and 35% for the S-type binder and C-type bin- der, respectively. To examine the effect of the S/B ratio on the strength development of Ca(OH) 2- based AA GGBS mortar with auxiliary activators, 12 mixes were tested for group III, as listed in Table 4 . To avoid a mix that is too lean, W/B ratios were selected to be 35% for mortar specimens with S-type binder and 40% for mortar specimens with C-type binder. A polycarboxylate-based high-range water-reducing admixture was added by 1% relative to the binder by weight in mortar mixes. 3.3. Test procedure The ﬂow of mortar mixes was recorded immediately after mixing according to the test procedure speciﬁed in the Korean Industrial Standard (KS L 5111) [10]. The compressive strength of the pastes and mortars was measured using cubes of 50 mm in size at the different ages of 1, 3, 7, 28, 56 and 91 days according to KS L 5105 [10]. To examine the effect of curing circumstances on the compressive strength development and hydration products of the Ca(OH) 2-activated GGBS mor- tars, both air-dried curing at room temperature and water curing at 21 /C176C condi- tions were used in all groups. To evaluate the hydration products and reactivity mechanism of the Ca(OH) 2-activated GGBS binder, XRD and EDX analyses combined with an SEM image were carried out on specimens of group I at the ages of 1, 3, 7, 28 and 91 days. XRD patterns were recorded on a Philips X’pert pro-MRD spectrometer using Cu K a Radiation with a scanning rate of 4.2 /C176/min from 7 /C176to 70/C176(2h). An EDX analysis was carried out using a Horiba EDX system at the accelerating voltage of 150 kV. A Hitachi S-4800 ﬁeld emission gun SEM was also used to observe the sam- ples at the accelerating voltage of 1 kV. Samples for S-4800 analysis were placed on the sample holders supported by carbon paint followed by a 1-min sputter coating of Pt. 4. Test results and discus

4.1. Hydration products by XRD analysis The XRD patterns of pastes measured at different ages and cur- ing conditions are shown in Figs. 1–3 . The compressive strength of the pastes is also listed in Table 2 . The XRD patterns for virgin GGBS show three characteristic peaks with the highest diffraction peak at a diffraction angle of 30 /C176(2h) [12]. The hydrated AA GGBS displayed diverse XRD patterns with evolved peak intensities according to ages and curing conditions, indicating a substantially different XRD pattern from the virgin GGBS. At age of 1 day, an amorphous hump was only observed at 2 h /C25 22.5 degrees on the XRD patterns of Ca(OH) 2-activated GGBS pastes without auxiliary activators, regardless of curing conditions, showing no diffraction peaks with steeple points through the measured diffraction angels. However, XRD patterns of Ca(OH) 2-based GGBS pastes with auxil- iary activators showed a distinct steeple peak consisting of calcium silicate hydrate (CaO/C1 SiO2/C1 H2O, CSH) gel [6], which can be regarded as a main hydration product, that began to appear at 2 h /C25 30 de- grees after the age of 1 day. The diffraction peaks of the CSH gels were more intensiﬁed in water curing than for air-dried curing; in particular, a notable difference of the intensity according to cur- ing condition was observed in Ca(OH) 2-activated GGBS without auxiliary activator. Compared with Ca(OH) 2-activated GGBS with- out the auxiliary activators, the Ca(OH) 2-based GGBS with auxil- iary activators also showed considerably more intensiﬁed diffraction peaks, indicating a slightly higher peak in Ca(OH) 2 and Na2SiO3-activated GGBS than in Ca(OH) 2 and Na 2CO3-activated GGBS. Hence, there is no doubt that water curing and use of auxil- iary activators contribute to the acceleration of the reactivity be- tween GGBS and Ca(OH) 2. As a result, a higher strength can be developed, as given in Table 1. With the increase of age, the inten- sity of the diffraction peak for CSH gel gradually increased whereas the increasing rate decreased. This trend was independent of the curing condition and the addition of auxiliary activators. On the other hand, the minor hydration products, which had very weak diffraction peaks, of Ca(OH) 2-based GGBS changed with the type of auxiliary activators and the curing condition. For the Ca(OH)2-activated GGBS (O-type), 2CaO /C1 Al2O3/C1 SiO2/C1 8H2O( C 2ASH8) and 4CaO /C1 Al2O3/C1 13H2O( C 4AH13) were produced after 7 days and at 91 days, respectively, in the air-dried curing condition, while 2MgO/C1 3SiO2/C1 xH2O( M2S3Hx) also appeared after 7 days in the water curing. The minor hydration products of Ca(OH) 2-based GGBS with auxiliary activators had higher diffraction peaks and wider diffuse bands than Ca(OH) 2-activated GGBS without auxiliary activators. This observation was more prominent for water curing than for air-dried curing. This indicates that the use of auxiliary activators and water curing can lead to a higher compressive strength gain of the binder, as given in Table 2 . 4.2. Microstructures by SEM/EDX analyses Figs. 4–6 show SEM images of pastes tested in group I. In these ﬁgures, the average values of the main molar ratios obtained from EDX analyses for the different morphology of hydration products are also given. It should be noted that the average molar ratios were determined from 10 different points in the hydration prod- ucts. Numerous irregular hexagonal plates are generally found in the microstruc

The structure of Ca(OH) 2-based GGBS with auxiliary activators be- came stabilized while that of Ca(OH) 2-activated GGBS without auxiliary activators still showed the fractional phases of virgin GGBS. In addition, the morphological characteristics of micro- structures of Ca(OH) 2-activated GGBS without auxiliary activators seldom progressed after the age of 7 days. These differences were more prominent in water curing than in air-dried curing. Table 2 indicates that a higher strength developed in Ca(OH) 2-based GGBS with auxiliary activators than in Ca(OH) 2-activated GGBS without auxiliary activators and at water curing than at air-dried curing. In addition, the compressive strength of Ca(OH) 2-activated GGBS without auxiliary activators decreased at 91 days compared with 28-day strength. Thus, the growth and density of hydration prod- ucts were consistent with the strength results. The hydration products that were found easily in the SEM images were CSH gels and C 2ASH8 hydrates coagulated around the GGBS particles [10]. The molar Si/Ca ratio in the CSH gels gen- erally increased with the increase of hydration time, showing a higher Si/Ca ratio in water curing than in air-dried curing, and in Ca(OH)2-based GGBS with auxiliary activators than in Ca(OH) 2- activated GGBS without auxiliary activators. On the other hand, the molar Si/Ca ratio of Ca(OH) 2-activated GGBS without auxiliary activators cured at air drying slightly dropped at 91 days. Wang and Scrivener [13] showed that the average detected Si/Ca ratios of CSH gels with hydration time of 1 day are 0.77 and 1.06 in NaOH-activated GGBS and Na 2SiO3-activated GGBS, respectively. The Si/Ca ratio of Ca(OH) 2-activated GGBS was lower than that of sodium ion-based AA GGBS due to the introduction of Ca 2+ cations. On the other hand, the molar Si/Al ratio and Ca/Al ratio in the C2ASH8 hydrates were nearly independent of the hydration time after 3 days, but were inﬂuenced by the addition of auxiliary acti- vators, showing that those values were higher in Ca(OH) 2-based (a) Air-dried curing (b) Water curing 7Days 91Days CSH gel ( Si/Ca=0.85) CSH gel ( Si/Ca=0.91) A B B A A A A B A B A A C2ASH8 ( Si/Al=3.90 Ca/Al=4.90 Si/Ca=0.80 ) CSH gel (Si/Ca=0.97) CSH gel ( Si/Ca=0.61) 91Days 7Days C2ASH8 (Si/Al=4.16 Ca/Al=3.20 Si/Ca=0.71) CSH gel ( Si/Ca=0.67) C2ASH8 (Si/Al=4.28 Ca/Al=6.42 Si/Ca=0.63) CSH gel ( Si/Ca=0.86) A A BB A B A B A A A A A A B B CSH gel ( Si/Ca=0.95) 1Days 3Days 28Days CSH gel ( Si/Ca=0.72) C2ASH8 (Si/Al=4.58 Ca/Al=6.41 Si/Ca=0.82) 3Days 28Days CSH gel ( Si/Ca=0.89) C2ASH8 ( Si/Al=4.40 Ca/Al=5.45 Si/Ca=0.82 ) Fig. 5. SEM images of Ca(OH) 2 and Na 2SiO3-activated GGBS paste. K.-H. Yang et al. / Construction and Building Materials 29 (2012) 410–419 415

GGBS with auxiliary activators than in Ca(OH) 2-activated GGBS without auxiliary activators, regardless of curing condition. Alkalis play a critical role in determining the properties and strength of hydration product of AA GGBS. Although the role of Na 2SiO3 or Na2CO3 in the hydration process of Ca(OH) 2-based GGBS was not clearly ascertained, Na+ cation would play a role involving an inter- change with Ca 2+ cations [6]. This increases the Si/Ca ratio of CSH gels. The incorporation of alkalis into CSH gels increases as the Si/Ca ratio of CSH gels increases [6]. The CSH gels with a higher Si/Ca ratio seem to be favorable for enhancing the strength devel- opment of AA GGBS [6,10]. 4.3. Compressive strength at 28 days The effect of the W/B ratio and S/B ratio on the 28-day compres- sive strength (f 0 c ) of AA GGBS mortars tested is shown in Fig. 7. Sim- ilarly to the case of OPC concrete, f 0 c of Ca(OH) 2-based GGBS mortars decreased with the increase of W/B ratio, showing a simi- lar decreasing rate regardless of the type of auxiliary activators and curing condition. At the same W/B ratio, Ca(OH)2 and Na2SiO3-acti- vated GGBS mortars developed a higher f 0 c than Ca(OH) 2 and Na2CO3-activated GGBS mortars by an average of 130% and 120% for air-dried curing and water curing, respectively. In addition, f 0 c of the AA GGBS mortars cured in water was commonly higher than that of the companion mortars cured by air drying, as reported in the group I. The compressive strength of Ca(OH) 2-based GGBS mor- tars was also somewhat inﬂuenced by the S/B ratio. The compres- sive strength of mortars tested minimally increased when the S/B ratio increased up to 1.5, beyond which it gradually decreased and then remained constant when the S/B ratio was more than 2.5. This trend was independent of the type of auxiliary activators and curing condition. When the volume of ﬁne aggregate exceeds a limiting value, f 0 c of mortar generally decreases due to the increase of internal microcracks and voids between pastes and aggregate particles [14]. Based on Fig. 7b, the limiting S/B ratio is considered to be 1.5 in terms of f 0 c of Ca(OH) 2-based GGBS mortars. SEM images of Ca(OH) 2 and Na 2CO3-activated GGBS paste. 416 K.-H. Yang et al. / Construction and Building Materials 29 (2012) 410–419

4.4. Compressive strength development The compressive strength of air-cured pastes activated by the single Ca(OH)2 reduced after 56 days by an average of 15% of their own 28-day strength, whereas that of the Ca(OH) 2-based activated pastes with the auxiliary activators improved at a long-term age, as given in Table 2 . A valid addition of auxiliary activators can stabi- lize the hydration reactivity of Ca(OH) 2-activated GGBS pastes at a long-term age [6,10]. This results in improving the intensities of hydration products and strength gain of Ca(OH) 2-based GGBS pastes at long-term ages. On the other hand, the strength gain according to the age of Ca(OH) 2-based GGBS mortars with auxiliary activators was signiﬁcantly affected by the curing condition, as gi- ven in Tables 3 and 4 . The 1-day strength of air-cured AA mortar was generally less than 30% of its 28-day strength. The average val- ues of the strength gain ratio of 3-day to 28-day strength were 0.66 for Ca(OH)2 and Na2SiO3-activated GGBS mortars cured by air dry- ing, 0.56 for Ca(OH) 2 and Na2SiO3-activated GGBS mortars cured in water, 0.65 for Ca(OH) 2 and Na 2CO3-activated GGBS cured by air drying and 0.55 for Ca(OH) 2 and Na 2CO3-activated GGBS cured at water. On the other hand, the average values of the strength gain ratio of 91-day to 28-day strength were 1.09 for Ca(OH) 2 and Na 2- SiO3-activated GGBS mortars cured by air drying, 1.21 for Ca(OH) 2 and Na 2SiO3-activated GGBS mortars cured in water, 1.06 for Ca(OH)2 and Na 2CO3-activated GGBS cured by air drying and 1.15 for Ca(OH) 2 and Na 2CO3-activated GGBS cured in water. The strength gain ratio at the early ages of water-cured mortars was lower than that of the companion air-cured mortars but notably in- creased at 56 and 91 days showing a higher strength gain ratio at the long-term ages, regardless of the mixing proportions and the type of auxiliary activators. Water curing signiﬁcantly accelerates the consumption of activators in the Ca(OH) 2-based AA GGBS mor- tars at long-term age, which contributes to the increase of intensi- ties of hydration products [6,10]. The typical compressive strength development against the age of Ca(OH)2-based AA GGBS mortars with S/B ratio of 3.0 at different curing conditions is plotted in Fig. 8. Although, the increasing rate of compressive strength according to age was dependent on the type of auxiliary activators, curing condition and W/B ratio, the strength development curve could be generally represented as a parabola, which agrees with the common observation for OPC concrete [14,15]. This parabolic strength development of Ca(OH)2-based AA GGBS mortars can be suitably formulated using the following equation [15]: f 0 c ðtÞ¼ t A1 þ B1t f 0 c ð1Þ where f 0 c ðtÞ is compressive strength according to age t (in days). The strength development rate at early age and long-term age is criti- cally identiﬁed according to the variation of the parameters A1 and B1, respectively [15]. The lower the values of A1 and B1, the higher the compressive strength at early age and at long-term age, respectively. For the OPC concrete cured by air drying, it is 0 5 10 15 20 25 30 35 40 45 Water-to-binder ratio, W/B 28-day compressive strength (MPa) ' S-type : air drying S-type : water curing C-type : air drying C-type : water curing 2 and Na2SiO3-activated GGBS mortars cured at air drying Ca(OH)2 and Na2SiO3-activated GGBS mortars cured at water Ca(OH)2 and Na2CO3-activated GGBS mortars cured at air dryin

recommended that the values of A1 and B1 are given as 4.0 and 0.85 [15], respectively. The values of both parameters determined from nonlinear regression analysis of test results are presented in Tables 3 and 4 . All specimens have a high correlation coefﬁcient ( R2) more than 0.9, as listed in the same tables. With the increase of the W/B ratio, the parameter A1 increased, while B1 decreased, regardless of the type of the auxiliary activator and curing condition. On the same W/B ratio, a higher A1 was obtained for water-curing than air-dried curing, and in Ca(OH) 2 and Na2CO3-activated GGBS mortars than in Ca(OH)2 and Na 2SiO3-activated GGBS mortars. This agrees with the observations for strength gain rate discussed in the previous para- graph. When the W/B ratio was less than 0.4, the values of A1 and B1 for Ca(OH)2-based GGBS mortars with auxiliary activators cured at air drying were comparable to those of OPC concrete. On the other hand, the values for A1 and B1 are minimally affected by the S/B ratio, as given in Table 4 . 5. Predictions of compressive strength development 5.1. Simple equations For a further development for precast products and/or mixing design of Ca(OH) 2-based AA GGBS concrete, simple equations to predict f 0 c and the two parameters A1 and B1 in Eq. (1) were pro- posed based on nonlinear multiple regression (NLMR) analysis of test data. In establishing the basic models, empirical equations [16] Na ion-based AA GGBS mortars to predict the values for A1 and B1 and 28-day strength in Eq. (1) were expanded. Each variable considered was combined and tuned repeatedly by a trial and error approach as an optimum solution until a relatively higher correla- tion coefﬁcient was obtained. Compressive strength ( f 0 c ) at the age of 28 days was normalized by a reference strength ( f0) of 10 MPa for dimension analysis. Based on the NLMR analysis of the test re- sults, f 0 c =f0 of Ca(OH) 2-based AA GGBS mortars can be empirically expressed as follows: f 0 c fco ¼ 0:36 abð1 þ k1Þ0:05 ðW=BÞ1:35 "# 1:31 ð2Þ where a = 1.1 for Ca(OH) 2 and Na 2SiO3-activated GGBS and a = 1.0 for Ca(OH)2 and Na2CO3-activated GGBS, b = 1.0 for air-dried curing and 1 :7 W=B a /C16/C170:45 for water curing, and k1 ¼ð S=BÞ0:25 for S=B 6 2:0 and k1 =( S/B)/C0 0.5 for S/B > 2.0. The coefﬁcients of a, b and k1 which explain the effect of the type of auxiliary activator, curing condition and S/B ratio on f 0 c , respectively, are optimally determined from the regression analysis of test data. In addition, the two parameters A1 and B1 of Eq. (1) to predict the compressive strength development of Ca(OH)2-based AA GGBS mortars were predicted by NLMR analysis as follows: A1 ¼ 5:02 logba /C1 b1:5ðW=BÞ0:5cþ 5:14 ð3Þ B1 ¼/C0 0:19 logba /C1 b1:5ðW=BÞ0:5cþ 0:789 ð4Þ 5.2. Comparisons with test results Comparisons of test results and predictions according to age are shown in Fig. 9. The mean ( cm), standard deviation ( cs) and coefﬁ- cient of variation ( cv) of ratios between measured and predicted compressive strengths at different ages and curing conditions are also given in Table 5 . The predicted 28-day compressive strength using Eq. (2) were in good agreement with the test results, indicat- ing cm of 1.02 and cv of 0.148 for air-cured mortars and cm of 1.03 and cv of 0.118 for water-cured mortars. In addition, compressive strengths at different ages predicted using Eqs. (1)–(4) were mostly within ±85% of measured values, though 3-day compressive strength was un
